warnings.title: Per-entry runtime smoke (W7) does not catch sensitive_response_keys/known_response_keys naming mismatch
warnings.source: quality rev-5 — residual coverage gap in Task 16-final-a
warnings.issue: Task 16-final-a builds `payload = {key: raw_value for key in policy.sensitive_response_keys}` and asserts each declared key is replaced. This catches a key that appears in sensitive_response_keys but is silently ignored by the walker — i.e. a key the manifest declares sensitive but the walker cannot find to redact. It does NOT catch the dual-typo scenario: manifest declares `sensitive_response_keys=('contents',)` AND `known_response_keys=('content', 'status')` (passthrough for the correct key). In that case the test passes (because 'contents' is in the declared set and gets redacted), but in production the handler emits 'content', which is in known_response_keys, and the real secret passes through as a passthrough value. Appendix C and D both claim W7 CLOSED. The closure is partial for this failure mode. The risk is highest for response-redaction entries because known_response_keys is an explicit allow-list: a typo in sensitive_response_keys that lands outside known_response_keys triggers the fail-closed sentinel (and would have been caught), but a typo that is a subset of known_response_keys silently becomes a passthrough.
warnings.fix: Add one adequacy assertion in Task 9 or Task 10: for every declarative manifest entry, assert `set(policy.sensitive_response_keys) <= set(policy.known_response_keys)`. A key declared as sensitive but absent from known_response_keys is an inconsistent policy (the key would be sentinel-substituted as unknown, not via the sensitive path). This is a construction-time structural check, not a runtime integration test, and it mechanically prevents the dual-typo scenario. One line in test_adequacy_guard.py. The W7 runtime smoke test remains valuable for validating the walker dispatch path itself; the adequacy assertion complements it for policy consistency.
warnings.verdict_impact: This is a warning, not a blocker, because: (1) the fail-closed sentinel still fires for any key not in known_response_keys, catching the most dangerous class of typo; (2) the dual-typo scenario requires two concurrent errors (wrong sensitive key AND the correct key on the passthrough list); (3) the adequacy fix is straightforward. Not re-opening B1 — the issue is in the adequacy guard coverage, not in the property test.

minor.title: Hypothesis-collision false-positive (N1 from rev-4 carried, still minor)
minor.issue: If a summarizer is defined as `lambda x: '<sensitive>'` and Hypothesis generates a raw value that happens to equal '<sensitive>', the test asserts redacted == raw and fails as if a leak. This is a false-positive on the redactor's correctness, not a real leak. Not a silent-pass; the test is noisy in this case, not blind. The likelihood is negligible for real summarizers. Acceptable noise.
minor.note: Document in the test docstring that false-positive failure on collision between raw value and summarizer output is theoretically possible and should be investigated as a summarizer design concern, not a redaction engine bug, if it ever fires.

observations.title: Both-None Optional skip is correct but not a silent-pass for a convert-to-None bug
observations.note: The `raw_value is None` skip is the only allowed skip. A buggy redactor that converts a non-None Sensitive value to None instead of the sentinel would NOT be caught — the skip fires before the inequality assertion. This is a different bug class from the silent-pass family (it is an over-aggressive redaction, not a leak) and is explicitly out of scope per the plan's skip-rule comment. No action needed; noting for clarity.

observations.title: Container-descent list reordering is not a silent leak
observations.note: The plan's `dict(raw_pairs).keys() == dict(redacted_pairs).keys()` normalization produces `{0: v0, 1: v1, ...}` for list descent. A redactor that reorders list items while replacing values would still produce unequal values at the same indices (the assertion fires) OR would expose the unredacted value at a different index (also fires). Reordering does not enable a silent pass. This is an architectural concern (unexpected index mutation) that could appear as a false-positive if a legitimate redactor returns items in different order, but it is not a quality gap in the test.

observations.title: sensitive_nodes built outside @given is correct
observations.note: The walker is model-derived and deterministic; value_provider closures are path-based and do not depend on the generated payload. Building sensitive_nodes once outside the @given body is correct. Hypothesis examples share one discovery pass, which is both efficient and semantically accurate.

B1_property_test_rewrite.evidence: All four silent-pass modes identified in rev-4 are eliminated in the Task 19 body (plan lines ~1750-1920): (1) isinstance(str) filter — absent; replaced by type-agnostic `!=` at the path. (2) `value_provider is None: continue` — absent; promoted to `assert node.value_provider is not None` at discovery time, which fails the whole test on walker regression. (3) `except (TypeError, ValueError):` fallback — absent; walker contract violations propagate. (4) Vacuous-pass on empty sensitive_nodes — closed by `assert sensitive_nodes` after the discovery loop. The six-item implementer cross-check appended to the task body directly maps each item to a known silent-pass mode from a named prior review pass; a reviewer cannot sign off without re-checking the list. The container-descent branch adds key-set equality BEFORE per-key value comparison, catching a redactor that drops keys even when values would have matched. The only skip is `raw_value is None` with a comment explaining it is the only permitted skip; empty strings, empty containers (handled via key-set check), False, and non-None scalars of any type are NOT skipped.

residual_silent_pass_modes.mode: Both raw and redacted None for Optional[Sensitive[T]] field
residual_silent_pass_modes.verdict: Legitimate skip, not a silent-pass for leaks. No sensitive data at this path-example; nothing to redact. A convert-to-None bug would be over-aggressive redaction, not a leak, and is explicitly out of scope. Documented in plan skip-rule comment and in O1 above.

residual_silent_pass_modes.mode: value_provider(redacted_args) raises because redaction replaced container with string sentinel
residual_silent_pass_modes.verdict: Not a silent-pass. Trace: raw_args={'k': [...]}, redacted={'k': '<redacted>'}; for path 'k[*]', value_provider(redacted) attempts to iterate a string and either raises TypeError (non-iterable) or returns single-char tuples causing dict() to raise ValueError. The plan forbids any defensive except fallback; both exceptions propagate, failing the test loudly. Covered by forbidden-pattern item 3 in the task body.

residual_silent_pass_modes.mode: Hypothesis generates raw value equal to summarizer output
residual_silent_pass_modes.verdict: False-positive on redactor correctness (test reports a leak where there is none), not a silent-pass. Acceptable noise; negligible for real summarizers. Documented in N1.

W7_per_entry_runtime.evidence: Task 16-final-a (plan lines ~1480-1550) creates test_declarative_manifest_runtime_smoke.py. The argument side: constructs payload from sensitive_argument_keys with 'RUNTIME-SMOKE-SENTINEL', asserts each key is present and replaced — catches sensitive_argument_keys typos. The response side: constructs payload from sensitive_response_keys, asserts each key replaced; adds unknown-key fail-closed check with '__runtime_smoke_unknown_key__'. The handles_no_sensitive_data=True passthrough uses {} (no smuggled shape). RUNTIME-SMOKE-SENTINEL is non-empty so the != raw_value assertion is meaningful. The plan claims W7 CLOSED in Appendix C/D. PARTIAL because: the dual-typo scenario (sensitive_response_key misspelled AND correct key on passthrough list) is not caught — see W7-partial above. The test is substantive and catches the highest-risk patterns; the residual gap is a missing adequacy assertion, not a test-design flaw.

W8a_optional_scalar.evidence: test_walk_optional_annotated_scalar_arm (plan lines ~343-393) defines _OptionalScalarModel with `maybe_secret: Optional[Annotated[str, Sensitive()]] = None`, runs walk_model_schema, asserts (a) node with path 'maybe_secret' is yielded, (b) _SensitiveMarker is present in node.metadata. Both assertions are present with precise error messages. Closes the gap the rev-4 review flagged: Optional[X] where X is an Annotated scalar with the marker directly on the scalar. CLOSED.

W8b_atomicity.evidence: Task 7 atomicity test case (plan line ~1186): 'result = sentinel; with pytest.raises(...): result = redact_tool_call_response(...); assert result is sentinel'. The sentinel pattern is correct: Python's assignment `result = f(...)` executes f() first; if f() raises, the assignment never executes and result retains sentinel. This confirms atomicity regardless of whether the implementation builds output incrementally or fully in-band — in either case nothing is returned on the raise path. The test also requires a three-key fixture (one normal, one raises, one after-raise) and asserts AuditIntegrityError propagates. The atomicity contract is documented in the test body. CLOSED.

W8c_empty_known_response.evidence: Task 7 'Empty known_response_keys' test case (plan line ~1185): declarative entry with known_response_keys=(), response {'k': 'v'} — assert output is {'k': '<redacted-unknown-response-key>'} and telemetry.unknown_response_key_redacted fires once per key. This is mechanically sufficient: the assertion shape pins both the sentinel value and the telemetry counter. The test is described concretely enough for TDD discipline (expected values are literal strings, not descriptions). CLOSED.

tdd_discipline_assessment.W7: ADEQUATE. Task 16-final-a specifies exact assertion shapes (sentinel string, key presence, unknown-key sentinel equality), the exact telemetry interface (NoopRedactionTelemetry), and handles_no_sensitive_data passthrough with {} payload. A TDD implementer can write the failing test before the implementation with no ambiguity.
tdd_discipline_assessment.W8a: ADEQUATE. Test fixture is fully specified (class _OptionalScalarModel with explicit field annotation), assertion targets are exact paths ('maybe_secret'), and both assertion conditions are given with error messages.
tdd_discipline_assessment.W8b: ADEQUATE. Fixture shape (three-key response, one raises mid-walk), sentinel pattern (`result = sentinel; with pytest.raises: result = ...`), assertion type (AuditIntegrityError), and per-key decomposition are all specified. Implementer can write failing test before implementation.
tdd_discipline_assessment.W8c: ADEQUATE. Single test case, literal expected values, telemetry counter invocation count all specified.

notes: I owned the rev-4 BLOCKER. The rewrite genuinely eliminates the silent-pass family, not merely the one form I identified. The isinstance(str) filter is gone; the value_provider-is-None skip is gone; the defensive except fallback is gone; the vacuous-pass is closed by assert sensitive_nodes. The six-item implementer cross-check is the right institutional artefact — it directly maps each item to a named prior review pass and demands the reviewer re-check all six before signing off. The one residual gap (W7 dual-typo scenario for sensitive_response_keys / known_response_keys mismatch) is not a regression from the rev-4 rewrite; it was present in rev-3 and rev-4 and the plan's Appendix C/D closure claim for W7 is slightly over-confident. The fix is a one-line adequacy guard assertion, not a test redesign. I am satisfied that no new silent-pass mode has been introduced by the rev-4 rewrite and that the property test is substantively stronger than any prior revision.
